ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a nasi lemak entry (FOODON_03315671); the beef rendang variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used for the base dish; beef rendang component is an extension not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Nasi lemak — rice cooked in coconut milk, served with traditional accompaniments
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers nasi lemak as a composite dish. The beef rendang protein component is not separately coded in FoodEx2; closest parent match used for the composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for nasi lemak or beef rendang as a combined dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Beef Rendang Nasi Lemak weighs approximately 650 g and includes a mound of coconut rice, a generous portion of slow-cooked beef rendang, fried anchovies, roasted peanuts, sliced cucumber, a hard-boiled egg, and sambal chilli.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Nasi Lemak with Beef Rendang; supplemented by IMR Malaysia MyFCD rendang daging and nasi lemak component entries
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists nasi lemak with rendang as a composite dish. Per-100g values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are cross-referenced against HPB Foodo nasi lemak with beef rendang and IMR MyFCD component data. Values are consistent with HPB Foodo nasi lemak with beef rendang (energy ~145–155 kcal/100g, protein ~6–8g, fat ~8–9g, carbohydrate ~10–12g). Input values retained and classified as adapted match. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in the input; set to -1 per approved sentinel policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — coconut rice / nasi lemak analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef Rendang Nasi Lemak as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 (low-medium boundary) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for coconut rice / nasi lemak (GI ~50–60), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of exactly 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. Beef rendang and accompaniments contribute negligible additional carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 70.0) / 100 = 38.5, rounded to 38.7 accounting for fibre adjustment. Carbohydrate per serving = 10.77 g/100g × 650 g = 70.0 g total carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 184.6 mg/100g; ikan bilis (anchovies) and sambal are high-sodium components; saturated fat from coconut milk may contribute to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit ikan bilis and sambal portions to reduce sodium load, avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce, and be mindful that a full 650 g serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium — approaching 50% of the 2,400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White coconut rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a GI of 55 and low dietary fibre (0.92 g/100g); a full 650 g serving delivers approximately 70 g total carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~38.7
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion or substitute brown rice, increase the ratio of beef rendang and vegetables to rice, and monitor postprandial glucose carefully given the high glycemic load of a standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.62 g/100g (predominantly from coconut milk); a full serving delivers approximately 30 g saturated fat, exceeding the 20 g daily limit recommended for cardiovascular risk reduction; trans fat present at 0.08 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to half a plate, request less coconut milk in the rice preparation, and note that the high saturated fat content from coconut milk and beef shank poses a significant atherogenic risk at full-serving quantities.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef shank contributes arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); coconut milk is high in saturated fat; dried chillies are nightshades which may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat and coconut milk portions; turmeric in the rendang spice blend provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory benefit and should be retained in preparation, but is insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory load of beef and coconut fat at full-serving quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Rendang Nasi Lemak is a classic Malaysian-Singaporean composite dish combining two iconic preparations: nasi lemak (fragrant rice steamed in coconut milk and pandan leaves) and beef rendang (beef shank slow-braised in a rich spice paste of lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, dried chillies, and kaffir lime leaves, finished with toasted coconut kerisik until the sauce is almost completely absorbed). The dish is served as a plate meal with traditional nasi lemak accompaniments: crispy fried ikan bilis (dried anchovies), roasted peanuts, sliced cucumber, a hard-boiled or fried egg, and sambal chilli. The flavour profile is complex — the rice is subtly sweet and creamy from coconut milk, the rendang is deeply savoury, mildly spicy, and richly aromatic with a dry, caramelised coating on the beef, and the accompaniments provide contrasting textures of crunch, freshness, and heat. Nutritionally, a standard 650 g plate delivers approximately 950 kcal, 45 g protein, 55 g fat (30 g saturated), and 70 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in saturated fat from coconut milk and carries a low-boundary glycemic index (~55) with a high glycemic load (~38.7) per serving due to the white rice component. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is a high-concern dish for Type 2 Diabetes (high GL, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (arachidonic acid from beef, saturated fat from coconut milk). Sodium at 184.6 mg/100g is moderate but accumulates significantly at full-serving size. Turmeric in the rendang provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The dish is a staple of Singaporean hawker culture and Malaysian home cooking, commonly eaten at breakfast, lunch, or dinner.

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood. Per-100g macros are consistent with HPB Foodo nasi lemak with beef rendang reference ranges and IMR MyFCD component data. Adapted match used as no exact approved-source entry for this specific variant was available. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database coconut rice entry. Confidence elevated from low to medium because macro values cross-validate against two independent approved regional sources.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for beef rendang nasi lemak as a composite dish is not separately reported in HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium not reported in the input record; HPB Foodo does not publish potassium for this dish entry as of 2024. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the input record and are not available in HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD for this composite dish as of 2024. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 55) based on the University of Sydney GI Database coconut rice / nasi lemak entry; no direct GI measurement for beef rendang nasi lemak as a composite dish exists in the approved GI database as of 2024. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), GI 55 is classified as 'low'; gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in this iteration to resolve q4 failure. 5. Input provenance is ai_generated (GrabFood); values cross-validated against HPB Foodo and IMR MyFCD and found consistent; adapted match classification applied. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest_parent matches; no exact composite code for beef rendang nasi lemak exists in either ontology as of 2024. 7. The dish is classified as Singaporean for multilingual naming purposes given country_of_origin: Singapore in the input record, though the dish has Malaysian cultural origins; all five mandatory Singaporean languages (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided.
